tweets.text:
The thing people are missing on GLM 5.2

Before it would have been kind of useless to rent H200s bc the open models weren’t good and codex subsidies so high no practical need to 

But subsidies getting cut. And GLM 5.2 is good enough to want *way* more tokens bc looping doesn’t break 

Not to mention god knows what data you’re going to lose now the companies are retaining your data more for “national security”. 

Completely changes economics in structural semi permanent way and it’s worth noting each of these points are likely to accelerate 

1) looping (massive token consumption with sub agents) will get better with model capability and base error rate bc compounded error 
2) coding plan subsidies will get cut as pressure builds into IPOs to show margin improvement 
3) national security concerns will increase causing larger data retention windows and more IP risk and perverse incentives (Anthropic building the software you use Claude code to build)

Providers like Vast are already seeing insane hockey stick acceleration that started in Feb and is probably going to hit a parabola 

This creates a flywheel for discount and efficiency providers which are highly international by nature (Malaysia bought billions of GPus, Middle East)

It’s worth repeating: prior to glm 5.2 this was all kind of theory / LARP. Just bc the open source models got brutally outperformed by close source 

I’m incredibly jacked up. Rising tide lifts all boats. GPUs. Chip Marketplaces. Chip financing. Crypto mining. The future is now

tweets.text:
Here's my current view on HYPE. Been working on it for a month or so now and still have more questions than answers.  Always learning.

My view is HYPE price is a function of 

1. growth in global trading volumes in general =V
Which is composed of 
crypto volume growth =VC
Trad FI volume growth = VT

2. Share of trading volume = S and correspondingly SC and ST

Given the HYPE protocol it's pretty easy to make a bull case for say $400 based on both V and S growth.

At $400 further upside seems highly limited in that Hyperliquid would have so much share that further upside is impossible (something like 100x of current revenues) yet the price increase would result in basically flat net coins outstanding or a deflationary inflationary equilibrium.  

So I get the bull case.  

As of today my assessment is 

VC growth expectations are radically too high.  
VT growth is boring
Hyper liquid share of SC is pretty darn big already 
Hyper liquid share or ST is almost nonexistent so does represents real opportunity. 

However ST currently is NOT in anyway relevant to tradfi in any meaningful way and is basically for crypto native tradfi tourists and bucket shop customers wanting high leverage and non regular trading hours trading.  

I see no chance that the bucket shop customer base is durable and sustainable nor is it a sizable when compared to the overall market volumes and never will be. 

I see high barriers and competitive and regulatory headwinds for ST during regular trading hours AND willingness from existing tradfi vested interest to evolve and meet after trading hours trading volumes so I am very very bearish relative to expectations of real ST market share growth for hyper liquid 

At mid 70's I think HYPE is a call option.  If none of the ST gains are made the existing volumes support a 30 price.  This is where I wanted to buy PURR and missed it.

So it's a call option trading for 40 that could be worth 330 or zero. (Meaning it falls from 70's to 30's if is a dud). 

Is that a good buy?  Risk forty to make 330.  Meh.  Would I short it. Nah it's not crazy.   I'd love to buy at 35 in the next bear market or short at 100 in a blow off top speculative frenzy even if that occurs in a long term bull trend to 400.  For now no position. 

Continuing to get smarter (still mostly dumb as I'm sure I will be told as soon as I press send). Innovative disruptive things are worth watching regardless of their future. So I am doing that

tweets.text:
$MSTR. It's been 19 months since I started saying sell $MSTR and Buy $IBIT if you want BTC exposure. It's been based on three simple concepts.

1. There is no economic justification for the shares to be priced at a MNAV premium 

2. Any leverage the company has access to is more expensive than you can create on your own and that leverage cost is a strong downward pressure on MNAV

3. At some point (maybe already as of today) the company will have no access to capital markets for attractive issuance resulting in a peak of total BTC held which will then fall slowly over time to pay the expensive leverage cost.

While saying all those things I have never said MSTR will go to zero unless BTC drops below 15k BUT it will trade at a MNAV slowly increasing MNAV discount forever. 

Even if BTC rallies a lot the MNAV will go to a discount because of these factors.  

That means holding the shares is much worse than holding IBIT

tweets.text:
“Absolute power corrupts absolutely”

In 2022, Strategy was completely different structurally than it is today.

The debt was ~10% of its current size, converts years further out, and it was basically free to carry (~$40M/yr).

The software cash flow could easily cover the payments and represented a much larger % of the actual debt - so the Bitcoin didn't have to be touched to service it.

In 2022 - the option was practically free to hold.

And there weren’t any other options for retail exposure until spot Bitcoin ETFs were approved in January of 2024.

In 2025, Strategy completely shifted their strategy.

They started issuing senior preferred shares like $STRC as their primary means of raising capital to buy more Bitcoin. They now owe $1.7B+/yr of dividend payments in perpetuity.

They offered some of the best rates in the industry, promising a “cash-like” instrument with near risk free 11%+ APY.

How? And where does the yield come from?

Their plan is to issue more common (MSTR) shares using their ATM facility, and sell the Bitcoin they purchased with the money they borrowed to pay the people they borrowed from.

“If you don’t know know where the yield comes from - you are the yield.”

They also owe another $5.9B of convertible debt that they’ll need cash to fund over the course of the next two years.

That + the dividend payments means a minimum of $10B+ of cash they need to find in less than two years that will fully be paid by MSTR shareholders and proceeds from selling Bitcoin.

One of the issues with the size Saylor is flexing, is that today they hold 4.2% of the circulating supply vs 0.7% in 2022.

They're a much larger structural risk to BTC itself if they actually need to sell a decent portion for cash, and their impact for selling with any real size is much larger.

The size, the obligations, and the timeline are different this time around.

Strategy tries to sell MSTR to retail as “amplified bitcoin” which is another fancy term for leverage.

Leverage is great for making money if the asset you’re buying goes up - but it hurts you that much more on the way down.

I believe in Bitcoin.

If you’re reading this, you most likely do too.

As Saylor quoted -

“Bitcoin is an economic machine based on a truth machine poised to emerge as a freedom machine.”

So if you’re bullish on Bitcoin and believe in the message Saylor preached in his video - don’t buy some company’s stock who’s using it to drive their Ponzi scheme, just buy Bitcoin.

Don’t pay the bill for someone else’s greed.

And remember there’s no such thing as free yield.

No singular man or company will ever be the singular reason for its failure or its success.

BTC is the freedom to escape the failure of countries and corporations who mismanage their balance sheet and debt.

Not to fund them.

tweets.text:
The role of stablecoins changed over time.

They started as a way for crypto traders to avoid crypto volatility without leaving crypto. That was the main loop for years: buy USDT, sit in stables, move funds to another exchange or wallet, then rotate back into the next trade.

They were super useful in DeFi. But that's changed in recent times.

Stablecoins are being used more and more in sectors that most of us wouldn't connect to crypto, like:

• Payroll
• Stock settlement
• AI agent payments

They're clearly moving beyond trading and becoming part of how apps and businesses move money.

I'll use USD1 as an example ↓

1. Payroll

@Zebec_HQ is paying 65K+ workers globally through USD1.

They likely opted for this because the rails are faster and cheaper than what the banks are offering.

The big advantage here is speed, as workers get their salaries instantly.

This makes payroll less dependent on bank hours, transfer windows, and slow cross-border rails.

2. Stock settlement

Binance recently added USD1 as a settlement asset for stock trading.

I think people are underestimating how important this is.

Having the majority of this stablecoin's circulating supply on Binance (~87%), and now routing stock settlement through it, creates a powerful flywheel, where:

• Traders need USD1 to enter/exit positions
• Market makers need USD1 to provide liquidity
• Binance has more reason to expand USD1 across more products

As one of the first real tests for equities settling through stablecoins, this could become one of USD1’s biggest growth levers.

3. AI agent payments

@worldlibertyfi shipped AgentPay earlier this year, which lets AI agents move money within preset spending rules.

An agent can pay for APIs, compute, data, or services without needing a human to approve every small transaction.

This is a sector where stablecoins make the most sense to me.

Agents need money that is stable, programmable, always online, and capable of settling in seconds. USD1 is used inside that payment flow, where the whole loop runs without manual approval.

It essentially becomes working capital for any piece of software that needs to spend money on its own.

My take

The stablecoin race is no longer just about listings or liquidity. It’s about getting embedded into flows where money moves frequently:

Payroll, stock settlement, agents, perps margining, prediction markets.
You get the idea.

Once a stablecoin becomes part of how an app or business operates, replacing it can be a hassle. That's why first movers will end up with an edge.

For the record, I'm personally holding a portion of my stablecoin in USD1 and putting it to work across different venues.

Adoption over everything.

Disclosure: I'm a USD1 holder myself.

tweets.text: On this day in 1840, Samuel Morse received a U.S. patent for his electromagnetic telegraph. Before it, market prices traveled at the speed of a horse or a ship, and a commodity price in New York could take days to reach Chicago. Within a decade, brokers on Wall Street were receiving real-time quotes from across the country, and stocks, bonds, and commodities could be traded across state lines in minutes instead of days. Middlemen who had built careers on information delays saw their advantage vanish overnight. By 1866, the transatlantic cable linked London and New York, and financial markets on two continents moved in sync for the first time.

🇨🇳 🇺🇸 China may not have won the frontier AI race yet but it is clearly winning the usage, cost, and adoption battles.

📈 On OpenRouter, weekly usage of Chinese AI models has surged since the start of 2026 to the point where it now far exceeds US models on the platform. OpenRouter does not represent the entire AI market, nor the revenues of OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, or Microsoft, but it is a strong signal of developer/API usage meaning an actual form of demand.

➡️ Chinese models do not need to be the best in the world across every benchmark to become dangerous. They mainly need to be good enough, much cheaper, easy to integrate and capable of addressing the majority of use cases. For many companies, the priority isn't necessarily to access the most powerful model but to use a reliable, fast, affordable and scalable one.

⚠️ However, more tokens do not necessarily mean more revenue but this clearly shows that competitive pressure is rising. If Chinese models continue to gain usage, they could put pressure on pricing, margins, and the premium attached to closed US models. For me, the narrative that American models will capture everything is becoming too simplistic.

The next phase of AI will obviously be a monetization battle.

*FT link: https://t.co/QWF5aN8ZGS
*Open Router link: https://t.co/KuMCtPBjkV

tweets.text:
🇺🇸 The biggest question facing new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh isn’t where rates go next ⚠

➡ It’s whether AI is a productivity revolution happening now—or a productivity promise that markets are pricing in years too early.

The historical parallel is Alan Greenspan in the 1990s:
• In 1996, the Fed allowed strong growth to continue as productivity improved.
• In 1999, it tightened aggressively as markets overheated.

Warsh appears to believe we are in the 1996 scenario: productivity gains from AI will eventually expand supply and ease inflationary pressures.

Others argue that because everyone expects AI gains, companies and investors are spending ahead of those benefits, creating demand today while productivity arrives later. In that case, higher rates may still be necessary.

The central banking question of this decade may be simple:
Is AI already making the economy more productive, or is it simply making everyone spend more in anticipation of future productivity?

➡ That answer could determine the path of interest rates, inflation, and economic growth over the next several years.

*WSJ link: https://t.co/tZZU8TPPgR

For JPM, AI's long-term productivity potential is massive for the coming years  ⬇

*6/20 Hormuz Tanker Crossings — Post-MOU Momentum Plunging*

🚢🛢️Today's chart isolates Strait of Hormuz crossings by tanker class: Crude, Chemical, LNG, LPG. The initial post-deal surge is losing steam. After peaking at 15 tankers on June 18th, crossings dropped to 5 yesterday and just 3 in the past 24 hours.

Four factors are slowing the reopening:

1. Is it even open? Iran's joint military command declared the strait closed today citing Israeli ceasefire violations in Lebanon (per Tasnim). This directly contradicts the MOU signed days ago. Until there's clarity on whether the waterway is officially open or closed, operators have little incentive to risk a transit.

2. Mines. INTERTANKO estimates 80 naval mines remain in the Traffic Separation Scheme — the main shipping lane that used to handle 130+ transits/day. Until clearance is complete, vessels are limited to two narrow alternative corridors with a combined capacity of ~60-80 ships/day.

3. Navigation risks. INTERTANKO's Marine Director noted this week that GPS in the strait remains "utterly unreliable," degrading AIS position data and elevating collision and grounding risk in the narrower corridors.

4. Regulatory uncertainty. Iran's Persian Gulf Strait Authority now requires vessels to purchase Iranian-approved insurance and a passage permit to transit — but has not disclosed fees, terms, or counterparties. INTERTANKO says the requirement violates the MOU's 60-day no-toll provision.

The result: major operators including Maersk are maintaining transit restrictions despite the MOU. 

Our index tracks every commercial crossing via AIS transponder data, refreshed every 30 minutes.

Prediction market open interest — the sum of outstanding contracts that remain “open” in the market — has reached a record weekly high. It hit $1.48 billion in the week ending June 15, the second record-setting week in a row.

Unlike trading volume, which reflects daily activity, open interest tracks the capital that participants have deployed and which remains at risk. The recent surge represents a roughly sixfold increase over the past year, highlighting how users are maintaining longer-term financial positions and how prediction markets are maturing into durable market infrastructure.

Big events bring in the crowds, and more traders are staying to participate in a wide range of markets across politics, economics, culture, crypto, and more.

When I gave this speech in October 2022, Bitcoin traded near $20,000, Strategy held 130,000 BTC worth about $2.6 billion, and $MSTR was ~$24 split-adjusted. Weeks later, after Bitcoin fell below $16,000, our debt exceeded the combined value of our BTC and cash reserves by ~$300 million, and $MSTR fell into the $13 range by year-end.

We stayed focused, strengthened the company, and executed our strategy. Since then, Strategy has raised over $60 billion of additional capital and invested it in Bitcoin, adding more than 716,000 BTC. Today, our BTC and USD reserves exceed debt by ~$48 billion. Thank you to everyone who believed, endured, and took the long view.
